differential_diagnoses: Pyoderma Gangrenosum (PG) associated with PASH syndrome
next_steps: Perform an urgent skin biopsy of an active ulcer edge for histopathology (to look for neutrophilic dermatosis)
Consult dermatology for suspected Pyoderma Gangrenosum
Initiate high-dose systemic corticosteroids (e.g., prednisone or methylprednisolone)
Consult gastroenterology for evaluation of potential underlying inflammatory bowel disease (Crohn's) via imaging or endoscopy
Discontinue invasive procedures near lesion sites to avoid pathergy
Perform wound cultures and tissue biopsy for bacterial, fungal, and mycobacterial pathogens to definitively rule out infection

differential_diagnoses: PASH syndrome (Pyoderma gangrenosum, Acne, and Suppurative Hidradenitis)
Crohn's disease with extensive extraintestinal manifestations
PAPA syndrome (Pyogenic arthritis, Pyoderma gangrenosum, and Acne)
next_steps: Perform diagnostic skin biopsy of the active ulcer edge for histopathology and tissue culture to confirm neutrophilic dermatosis and exclude infection
Initiate systemic corticosteroids after obtaining microbiologic samples to treat presumptive pyoderma gangrenosum
Consult rheumatology and dermatology for management of autoinflammatory spectrum
Obtain inflammatory markers including ESR, CRP, and ferritin
Consult gastroenterology for colonoscopy or capsule endoscopy to evaluate for underlying subclinical Crohn's disease
Avoid unnecessary surgical debridement or invasive procedures due to the risk of pathergy
Perform genetic testing for PSTPIP1 gene mutations
